questions.questionText: Which of the following is an essential fatty acid?
questions.explanation: Humans cannot introduce double bonds past C-9, making linoleic acid (18:2) essential.

questions.questionText: The most abundant class of lipids in cell membranes is:
questions.explanation: Glycerophospholipids form the lipid bilayer of cell membranes.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is predominantly found in the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane and involved in apoptosis signaling?
questions.explanation: PS flips to the outer leaflet as an 'eat me' signal during apoptosis.

questions.questionText: Cholesterol is a precursor for all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Thyroid hormones are derived from tyrosine, not cholesterol.

questions.questionText: The basic structural unit of sphingolipids is:
questions.explanation: Ceramide (sphingosine + fatty acid) forms the core of sphingolipids.

questions.questionText: Which lipid contains an ether bond?
questions.explanation: Plasmalogens (e.g., in myelin and heart) have an ether-linked alkenyl chain at C-1.

questions.questionText: Arachidonic acid is a precursor for:
questions.explanation: Arachidonic acid (20:4) is the main precursor for eicosanoids.

questions.questionText: High levels of which lipoprotein are inversely associated with the risk of coronary artery disease?
questions.explanation: High-Density Lipoprotein (HDL) mediates reverse cholesterol transport.

questions.questionText: Cardiolipin is characteristically found in the:
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin is unique to mitochondria and essential for ETC function.

questions.questionText: A fatty acid with the nomenclature 18:2(delta 9,12) is:
questions.explanation: Linoleic acid has 18 carbons and two double bonds at positions 9 and 12.

questions.questionText: In Chemistry of Lipids, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Biochemistry texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Biochemistry references for Chemistry of Lipids. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Chemistry of Lipids.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Chemistry of Lipids scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Chemistry of Lipids.
